sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A health psychology study followed 200 first-year medical students during an exam block. Students wore wrist sensors and provided 8 AM saliva samples for cortisol. Those in the lowest third of social support reported mean stress of 8.1/10 and mean cortisol of 19 nmol/L. Those in the highest third of support reported mean stress of 5.9/10 and cortisol of 14 nmol/L. The study measured naturally occurring support networks rather than assigning support.
questions.stem: What is the best interpretation of support and stress?

choices.D: Higher support was associated with lower stress and cortisol.

explanations.A: Random assignment allocates participants to conditions by chance. The stem may ask about measurement or observational limitations rather than an experimental solution. The passage focus is support-stress association, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Positive reinforcement adds a consequence that increases behavior. The stem is asking about a different mechanism rather than an added reward increasing a response. The passage focus is support-stress association, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Difference threshold is the smallest detectable difference between stimuli. The passage item asks about a broader psychological or sociological concept. The passage focus is support-stress association, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Correct. The high-support group had lower self-reported stress and lower morning cortisol, but support was not assigned. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean perceived stress ratings and 8 AM salivary cortisol levels among students in the lowest versus highest social-support terciles.

questions.stem: Morning cortisol indexes activity of the:

explanations.A: The somatic nervous system controls voluntary skeletal muscle. Cortisol release is endocrine HPA-axis activity. The relevant evidence instead centers on cortisol stress biomarker.
explanations.B: The vestibular system detects balance and head motion. It does not regulate cortisol in the stress pathway. The relevant evidence instead centers on cortisol stress biomarker.
explanations.C: Correct. Cortisol is released through hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal stress signaling. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Mirror neurons are implicated in observing actions. They do not regulate cortisol release through the HPA axis. The relevant evidence instead centers on cortisol stress biomarker.

choices.B: Support networks were measured, not manipulated.

explanations.A: Continuous reinforcement rewards every target response. The described pattern is not a reward after every response. The passage focus is measured social support, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Correct. Students with different support levels may also differ in sleep, resources, workload, or coping style. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Semantic memory stores facts and meanings. The stem does not ask about general knowledge. The passage focus is measured social support, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Cultural capital includes valued skills, credentials, tastes, and knowledge. The passage item is not mainly about institutionalized cultural resources. The passage focus is measured social support, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Seeing the exam block as a challenge rather than threat is:

explanations.A: Correct. Primary appraisal is the initial evaluation of a stressor’s meaning. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: Secondary socialization is learning norms outside the family. Appraising an exam as challenge or threat is a stress-evaluation process. The relevant evidence instead centers on meaning assigned to exam stress.
explanations.C: Discrimination is unfair treatment based on group membership. The exam-block item asks how a student appraises a stressor, not unequal treatment. The relevant evidence instead centers on meaning assigned to exam stress.
explanations.D: Sensory transduction converts stimulus energy into neural signals. It does not describe interpreting an exam as a challenge. The relevant evidence instead centers on meaning assigned to exam stress.

questions.stem: Which result best supports a buffering effect?

choices.D: Exam stress predicts cortisol less strongly among high-support students.

explanations.A: Habituation is decreased response to repeated stimulation. The passage item does not describe reduced response after repeated harmless exposure. The passage focus is support as stress buffer, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: The bystander effect is reduced helping when other potential helpers are present. The passage item may concern effort, conformity, or another social process. The passage focus is support as stress buffer, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Medicalization defines nonmedical problems as medical issues. The passage does not describe expanding medical labels onto social life. The passage focus is support as stress buffer, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Correct. A buffer reduces the strength of the stress-outcome relationship when support is high. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.passage: A clinic evaluated a stress-management workshop for caregivers of relatives with dementia. It taught problem-focused coping for modifiable stressors, emotion-focused coping for unavoidable stressors, and mindfulness. Before the workshop, caregivers spent 6.5 hours/week arranging appointments and 9.0 hours/week managing repetitive questions. Checklists reduced appointment problems; acceptance and breathing reduced distress from repetitive questioning. Caregivers with higher internal locus of control completed more workshop activities.
questions.stem: Scheduling checklists for appointment problems are:

explanations.A: Emotion-focused coping regulates distress. It is less appropriate when the stressor can be directly changed through planning or action. The passage focus is modifiable appointment stressor, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Class consciousness is awareness of shared class position and interests. It does not describe a caregiver checklist used to solve appointment logistics. The relevant evidence instead centers on modifiable appointment stressor.
explanations.C: Correct. The checklist changes a modifiable practical stressor rather than only regulating emotion. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Spontaneous recovery is reappearance of an extinguished conditioned response after time. It does not describe caregiver scheduling. The relevant evidence instead centers on modifiable appointment stressor.

questions.stem: Acceptance and breathing for repetitive questioning are best because the stressor is:

explanations.A: A fixed-ratio schedule rewards behavior after a set number of responses. The passage does not describe a predictable response-count reward rule. The passage focus is unavoidable caregiver stressor, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Correct. When a stressor cannot be easily changed, regulating emotional response can be adaptive. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Formal norms are codified rules. Repetitive questioning by a relative with dementia is a caregiving stressor, not a written social rule. The relevant evidence instead centers on unavoidable caregiver stressor.
explanations.D: A primary reinforcer satisfies a biological need. A phone tone predicting a message is a learned cue, not a biological reward. The relevant evidence instead centers on unavoidable caregiver stressor.

questions.stem: Internal locus of control means believing:

choices.A: One’s own actions can influence outcomes.

explanations.A: Correct. Caregivers with this belief may complete activities because they expect their efforts to matter. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: Selection bias occurs when group membership or participation is systematically nonrandom. The stem does not describe self-selection or volunteer differences. The passage focus is belief about personal control, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Stigma is negative labeling or devaluation of a person or group. The stem does not describe discrediting or stereotyping a marked identity. The passage focus is belief about personal control, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Sensory adaptation is reduced receptor responsiveness to constant stimulation. The item centers on cognition, social context, or decision processes instead. The passage focus is belief about personal control, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Which workshop rule best applies coping theory?

choices.D: Match coping strategy to stressor controllability.

explanations.A: Group polarization is movement toward more extreme views after group discussion. The passage does not describe attitude extremity after discussion. The passage focus is strategy-stressor match, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Social reproduction is the transmission of inequality across generations. The stem does not trace class advantage across generations. The passage focus is strategy-stressor match, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: CBT targets maladaptive thoughts and behaviors. The stem is not asking about structured psychotherapy. The passage focus is strategy-stressor match, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Correct. Problem-focused strategies fit changeable demands; emotion-focused strategies fit less changeable demands. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.stem: Which outcome would show mindfulness improved emotion regulation?

choices.C: Less distress during repeated questioning without fewer questions.

explanations.A: Operant conditioning changes behavior through reinforcement or punishment. It is not the best fit when the stem asks about group pressure, appraisal, validity, or social structure. The passage focus is distress despite unchanged stressor, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Population aging is a rising proportion of older adults. The stem is not about age structure driving social needs. The passage focus is distress despite unchanged stressor, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Correct. Emotion regulation changes the response to the stressor even if the stressor continues. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Discriminant validity means weak relation with unrelated measures. The passage does not use an unrelated comparison measure. The passage focus is distress despite unchanged stressor, so this option is less directly supported.
